title: The Reluctant Fighter

jokes.text: Would you settle for Tony Banta... the bum?
jokes.setup: Someone asks for Tony Banta the boxer
jokes.punchline: Dispatcher offers Tony Banta the bum instead
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with good word economy. The contrast between 'boxer' and 'bum' lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: I'd like to speak to Tony Banta the boxer.

dialogue.speaker: Louie DePalma
dialogue.line: Tony Banta the boxer? We don't have a Tony Banta the boxer here. But we got Tony Banta the bum. You want him instead?

jokes.text: You coming into my garage, you gonna leave me a dollar deposit.
jokes.setup: Visitor comes to the garage
jokes.punchline: Louie demands a deposit just for entering
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment - petty, money-grabbing, and absurd. Well-integrated character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey! Where do you think you're going? You can't just walk in here!

dialogue.speaker: Visitor
dialogue.line: I'm just looking for someone.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That'll be five dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Visitor
dialogue.line: Five dollars? For what?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Deposit. You wanna walk around in here, you put down five bucks. You break something, I keep it. You don't break nothing, I give it back.

jokes.text: It's non-refundable. That's okay, as long as I get it back.
jokes.setup: Louie says the deposit is non-refundable
jokes.punchline: Vince agrees as long as he gets it back
jokes.explanation: Classic contradiction joke - agreeing to non-refundable while expecting a refund. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The deposit is non-refundable.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: Okay, as long as I get it back.

jokes.text: and you were the sixth one that came to my mind.
jokes.setup: Vince lists Tony's qualifications as a fighter
jokes.punchline: Reveals Tony was only his sixth choice
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - builds up Tony's qualifications then deflates with the sixth choice reveal. Great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: Tony's got all the qualifications of a great fighter. He's got the speed, he's got the moves, he's got the killer instinct.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: You know, I had my first five choices, but they all turned me down.

jokes.text: Boy, it's kind of like a painter getting a chance to beat up on Rembrandt.
jokes.setup: Tony compares fighting a great boxer to artistic opportunity
jokes.punchline: Mixing metaphor of artistic inspiration with literal violence
jokes.explanation: Clever mixed metaphor that's both unexpectedly intellectual for Tony and absurdly literal. Shows character depth while being genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, fighting a great boxer is like... it's like a great artist getting to paint with Picasso, or a musician getting to jam with Miles Davis.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: It's the opportunity of a lifetime to create something beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Of course, in my case, what we're creating is a black eye and a broken nose.

jokes.text: The stalls in the bathroom... They got doors.
jokes.setup: Tony expects the ex-champ's gym to be classy
jokes.punchline: The height of class is bathroom stall doors
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about low standards of 'class' in boxing gyms. Economical delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I expected the ex-champ's gym to be more... classy.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: Classy? The height of class is bathroom stall doors.

jokes.text: I think I'll stick with nervous.
jokes.setup: Bobby shows Tony a tension-relieving exercise
jokes.punchline: Tony's deadpan rejection after seeing whatever ridiculous thing Bobby did
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction beat - we don't see the exercise but Tony's response tells us everything. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: It's a tension-relieving exercise. You put your hands on your shoulders like this, and you rotate your head in a circular motion.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Hey, we make a great team, don't we? I mean, with my muscle and his brains, we can't lose. It's the other way around, Vince. Right.
jokes.setup: Vince boasts about their complementary strengths
jokes.punchline: Tony corrects him - Vince has the brains, Tony has the muscle
jokes.explanation: Standard but well-executed character confusion. Shows both Tony's self-deprecation and Vince's obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: You know, Tony, we make a great team. I've got the brains, you've got the brains.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You got the brains, I got the muscle.

jokes.text: I was married. Close enough.
jokes.setup: Vince asks if anyone has worked a fight
jokes.punchline: Alex equates marriage to boxing experience
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical joke that compares marriage to combat. Universal relatability with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: Has anybody here ever worked a fight?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I was married once.

jokes.text: His accountant and his lawyer? His Tuesday and his Wednesday.
jokes.setup: Elaine suggests the women might be professionals
jokes.punchline: Alex escalates to naming them by days of the week
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke that builds on the setup effectively. The 'days of the week' punchline is both clever and cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You think they're professionals?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, they've got to be Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday. Saturday and Sunday are their days off.

jokes.text: Oh, I finally made it. Mistaken for cheap.
jokes.setup: Benny introduces his women friends to Tony's friend Elaine
jokes.punchline: Elaine's sarcastic reaction to being lumped with the other women
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Elaine's wit and dignity. The ironic 'made it' works well.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh great, so I'm just one of the girls now?

jokes.text: Nice shot of the wheelchair, ain't it?
jokes.setup: Louie shows Tony newspaper coverage of the press conference
jokes.punchline: Focuses on the wheelchair photo as if it's good publicity
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Louie's callousness and opportunism

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look at this! You're on the front page of the newspaper!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's great, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Great? It's fantastic! Look at this photo - you in the wheelchair. This is perfect publicity!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Louie, I'm not sure that's the angle we want to emphasize.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? It's sympathetic, it's human interest. The people eat this stuff up! You're gold, Tony. Pure gold!

jokes.text: That's it... I know what I got to do. I'm going to give that kid my kidney.
jokes.setup: Tony feels bad about fighting against a sick kid's hero
jokes.punchline: His solution is to donate an organ
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony moment - his heart is in the right place but his solution is completely absurd. Shows character beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm gonna donate an organ.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tony, that's beautiful. Which organ?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The jukebox in my apartment.

jokes.text: Sure, you've come this far without a brain.
jokes.setup: Tony says he can get along without a kidney
jokes.punchline: Louie points out he's already functioning without a brain
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie insult with perfect timing and structure. Builds logically on Tony's statement.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well you're already functioning without a brain.

jokes.text: I look over at my sofa, I see the Rodriguez fight. I look over at my armchair, I see the Jefferson fight. My little end tables are the Camillio brothers.
jokes.setup: Louie explains how betting against Tony furnished his apartment
jokes.punchline: Each piece of furniture represents a different Tony loss
jokes.explanation: Brilliant extended metaphor that builds beautifully. Creative and cruel in equal measure, perfectly Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You see this chair? Tony lost $200 on that one. This couch? $500. That lamp over there? $150. Every single piece of furniture in this apartment came from betting against Tony.

jokes.text: Wait a minute, wait a minute, let's make that a 27-inch. Ma's eyes ain't what they used to be.
jokes.setup: Louie increases his bet
jokes.punchline: Justifies it with concern for his mother's eyesight
jokes.explanation: Nice character touch showing Louie's twisted family consideration

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm doubling my bet.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: My mother's eyesight is getting worse. She's gonna need thicker glasses.

jokes.text: You know how when you turn over one of those rocks and you see those slimy, crawly things underneath? Louie makes those things go 'yuck.'
jokes.setup: Alex describes finding disgusting things under rocks
jokes.punchline: Louie is so repulsive he disgusts even those creatures
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that builds to a perfect punchline. The image is vivid and the logic is sound while being absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, I was looking under rocks today, and I found the most disgusting things — slugs, worms, insects. But you know what? Even they were repulsed by Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey! What's that supposed to mean?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It means you're so repulsive, you disgust creatures that live under rocks.

jokes.text: We all know that when a loser wants to win, naturally he loses. It's a law of the universe. Banta's proven this time and time again. But... when a loser wants to lose...
jokes.setup: Louie develops a theory about Tony wanting to lose
jokes.punchline: Creates pseudo-logical framework for betting
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pseudo-intellectual reasoning that's both absurd and logical within Louie's worldview

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I got it all figured out. Tony wants to lose. That's why he keeps losin'. It's psychological.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So if a guy wants to lose, he's gonna lose. Which means if I bet against him, he wins. But he WANTS to lose, so he loses anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So I should bet FOR him, which makes him want to lose more, so he loses, which means I win!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Unless he knows that I know that he wants to lose, in which case he'll win just to spite me.

jokes.text: the only way that he can stay a loser is to win.
jokes.setup: Alex's complex logic about Tony wanting to lose
jokes.punchline: The paradoxical conclusion that he must win to stay a loser
jokes.explanation: Clever paradox that's both logical and absurd. The pseudo-intellectual analysis is funny in its complexity.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: If Tony wants to lose, and he wins, then he's not doing what he wants. But if he loses, he's doing what he wants, which means he's not really trying to lose, so he wins. But if he wins, then he's not doing what he wants...

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: My head hurts.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The only way for Tony to stay a loser is to win.

jokes.text: Okay, okay, do you think we should get out of NATO?
jokes.setup: Tony asks to talk about something other than boxing
jokes.punchline: Bobby suggests discussing NATO withdrawal
jokes.explanation: Great non-sequitur that's both random and somehow perfect for the moment. The specificity makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Can we talk about something other than boxing for once?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Sure, why not? Let's discuss NATO withdrawal.

jokes.text: What's NATO?
jokes.setup: Bobby's earlier NATO question
jokes.punchline: Tony doesn't know what NATO is
jokes.explanation: Good character moment and callback. Shows Tony's limited worldview while providing closure to the NATO bit.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: NATO? What's NATO?

jokes.text: You want to ask Tony anything else, hail his cab. That way he gets to put it on the meter.
jokes.setup: Reporters want to ask Tony more questions
jokes.punchline: Alex suggests they pay for his time by taking his cab
jokes.explanation: Clever way to reference Tony's day job while making a practical point about his time being valuable.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Why don't you guys take his cab? He gets paid by the mile.

jokes.text: He still can't count to three, but he'll be okay.
jokes.setup: Asked how the champ is doing after being knocked out
jokes.punchline: Reference to the ten-count but minimizing it
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever reference to brain damage from boxing. The casualness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: He's fine. The ref only counted to eight.

jokes.text: Benny lost to a hack?
jokes.setup: Tony explains he drives a cab for a living
jokes.punchline: Brian plays on 'hack' as both cab driver and inferior boxer
jokes.explanation: Nice wordplay that works on multiple levels - cab driver and poor boxer. Economical and sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: A hack? You mean like a hack boxer?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, a hack cab driver.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Oh, that's what I said.

jokes.text: What'd you hit me with? The ball bearings from my wheelchair.
jokes.setup: Tony asks what Brian hit him with
jokes.punchline: Brian claims it was wheelchair ball bearings
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that works because it comes from the kid himself. Unexpected and specific detail makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What'd you hit me with?

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Wheelchair ball bearings.

jokes.text: Nah, I've seen your record.
jokes.setup: Tony offers to be Brian's new fighter hero
jokes.punchline: Brian rejects him based on his poor record
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan put-down from the kid that's honest but not cruel

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Tony, I appreciate the offer, but look at your record.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What's wrong with my record?

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: You've lost more fights than you've won!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I'm still standing.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Barely. I need a hero, not a cautionary tale.

jokes.text: I'll stick around until you lose one. That could be weeks.
jokes.setup: Brian agrees to be Tony's fan until he loses
jokes.punchline: Tony's self-deprecating response about losing soon
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's realistic assessment of his abilities. Self-deprecation works well.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Alright, I'll be your fan until you lose.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's great! Thanks for the support.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Better get comfortable, though — you're gonna be my fan for a long time.

jokes.text: Too much skating between fights.
jokes.setup: Brian didn't like his first hockey game
jokes.punchline: He wanted more fighting, less hockey
jokes.explanation: Clever reversal of expectations - complaining about the actual sport interrupting the fights. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He wanted more fighting, less hockey

jokes.text: I don't want to interrupt you, I like what you're saying.
jokes.setup: Louie is insulting Tony to Brian
jokes.punchline: Brian encourages Louie to continue the insults
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Brian's appreciation for Louie's meanness

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Go ahead, Louie. Don't stop now. You're just getting warmed up.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why should I waste my breath on this paluka?

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Because you're so good at it. Come on, let's hear some more.

jokes.text: I don't want to interrupt you, I like what you're saying.
jokes.setup: Louie is insulting Tony and calling him names
jokes.punchline: Brian encourages Louie to continue the insults
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Brian's mischievous side while setting up the pickpocketing payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Don't stop now, Louie. You're just getting warmed up!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why should I? This mook doesn't deserve my time.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Come on, lay it on him. You were doing great!

jokes.text: How come you didn't punch him like you punched me? I did better... I got his wallet.
jokes.setup: Tony wonders why Brian didn't punch Louie
jokes.punchline: Brian reveals he pickpocketed Louie instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff that recontextualizes the earlier scene. Shows Brian's cunning while getting revenge on Louie. Perfect character integration and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Why didn't you punch him?

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: I pickpocketed him instead.

tmdb_overview: Tony faces a bout with a champ who has dedicated his fight to a disabled youth.
insight: Boxing subplot triggers 31 jokes in 45 minutes — Taxi's character-comedy sweet spot.
